chunk_summary: Hospitals and neonatal units must enforce strict probiotic procurement standards, including lot-wise quality assurance, to ensure safety for vulnerable populations. Governance should integrate adverse-event surveillance and align with regulatory frameworks like FSSAI guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of stringent probiotic procurement standards in neonatal and hospital settings.
Learn the role of lot-wise Certificates of Analysis (COA) in ensuring product quality and safety.
Recognize the regulatory framing of probiotics as foods under FSSAI guidelines and its implications for clinical counseling.
Explore the integration of adverse-event surveillance and registries in neonatal probiotic use.
key_facts: Hospitals should require probiotics to meet FSSAI labeling standards, including strain designation, end-of-shelf-life viability, and storage conditions.
Lot-wise Certificates of Analysis (COA) must document viable counts, microbial contamination screening, and label consistency.
Probiotic use for NEC prevention should be governed by unit policies with standardized eligibility, timing, and contraindications.
A simple registry tracking product lot, start/stop dates, feeding type, sepsis, NEC stage, and mortality supports safety monitoring.
FSSAI guidelines classify probiotics as foods, often for populations above 2 years, and prohibit medicinal claims unless explicitly permitted.
reference_quote: FSSAI directions for nutraceutical and related categories emphasize that covered products are foods (often framed for populations above 2 years) and carry 'not for medicinal use,' with disease cure or mitigation claims generally disallowed unless specifically permitted.
document_key_topics: Adverse-event surveillance in neonatal probiotic use
Governance of probiotics for NEC prevention
